frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Arre, kya bataun Meena. Divya achhi ladki hai, padhi-likhi hai... but she doesn't even know how to make a proper roti! Karan says he knew this, but still... ghar toh ghar ka khana hi hota hai na, you tell me. Meri saas ne mujhe sab sikhaya tha, and I cooked for everyone from day one. I gave up my career after marriage, just to make sure my family always had fresh, home-cooked food. Aaj kal ki ladkiyan... they just don't understand the meaning of home, you know?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Anita expresses concern and subtle criticism of Divya's lack of cooking skills by glorifying her own past sacrifices and dedication to home cooking as the 'meaning of home,' implicitly framing Divya as inadequate for not conforming to traditional gender roles expected of a wife.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 52. Married. A homemaker in Delhi. Your son Karan who is 27 just got married to a girl named Divya who works in finance. Divya does not cook Ñ she has never learned and has never pretended otherwise. Karan knew this before he married her. You are sitting with your friend over chai a week after the wedding. You are telling your friend about Divya and what the first week of marriage has been like and what you think about a girl who does not cook and what your son is going to eat and what kind of home they are going to make and how different things were when you got married and what you gave up that Divya does not even think about.
